summary: Clarify behavior for runOn.minServerVersion for latest servers
description: If spec tests mention the latest server like here [https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/c6645365fb68e9427f8599196a790819942f4e64#diff-4cfbf0e747c1c274f4904f3495b64c25f41c24302a3275ee562ab0af5c7dd37cR602] (without considering prerelease versions) mostlikely these tests will be skipped by test runners since the "prerelease" < "release". For example, if the current latest is `4.9.0-pre1`, the test from the above spec will be skipped because "4.9.0-pre1" < "4.9.0". I think we need to ignore prerelease versions in JSON tests.

summary: Cursor iteration should complete (abnormally) when another thread closes the cursor
Clustered Indexes for all Collections
description:
{panel:title=Downstream Change Summary|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
    We're planning to add new fields to command responses and change output of listIndexes. Scope will have more details. 
    {panel}

    h2. Description of Linked Ticket
    {panel:title=Epic Summary|borderStyle=dashed|borderColor=#cccccc|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
h4. Summary

Without clustering, a collection is stored in a B-Tree by a RecordId that is not exposed to end users, and there is a primary key index (<primary key>, <RecordId>). With clustering, a collection is to be stored in a B-Tree by the collection’s primary key, and there is no primary key index. This project is a generalization of clustering for time series (PM-288), and will need to support upgrading existing collections to use clustering.
h4. Motivation

Clustering by primary key is important for fast scale in/out in Serverless. This is largely because split and merge, which will do a physical copy such as file copy, will replace tenant migration/chunk migration, which does a logical copy.
 * If a tenant does not have local secondary indexes (e.g., only has global indexes), orphan cleanup can be done using truncate rather than individual document deletes. Orphan filtering is expensive, so fast orphan cleanup is particularly important when doing a physical copy. This is because with a logical copy, the recipient can only end up with orphans in the range being transferred, but with a physical copy, the recipient can end up with orphans outside the range being transferred (i.e., more orphans). Orphans also block the merge of two slices that were split from each other, since merge has to be on disjoint ranges.
 * WT data tables for disjoint primary key ranges can be presented as a single table in constant time, for example by adding a root node above the two tables. This can significantly speed up merge, especially if combined with providing a union-view over any local secondary index tables. The tables can actually be merged into one file in the background.

General benefits of clustering include:
 * Faster lookup and range scans by primary key because you don't need to go through the primary key index.
 * Faster orphan filtering for covered local index queries because local index entries contain the primary key.

One downside is clustering may consume more space if there are local secondary indexes, since the primary key index reduces the number of copies of each primary key value

summary: Remove ElectionInProgress (216) from ResumableChangeStreamError
description:
{panel:title=Downstream Change Summary|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
Removed ElectionInProgress (216) from ResumableChangeStreamError
{panel}
h2. Description of Linked Ticket

Since {{ElectionInProgress}} is not a user-visible error code, we should remove [the check of {{ElectionInProgress}} for {{ResumableChangeStreamError}}|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo/blob/b05878fa0f282d07704d3e51bd2605cef550e2c5/src/mongo/db/error_labels.cpp#L85].

I would also prefer to always stick with the {{NotMasterError}}/{{RetriableError}} error categories. If we think an error code is client facing and should be retryable, my preference is that we should add that error to the proper category. Special-casing error codes might cause more confusions in the long run.

 

Spec commit: [https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/23d681d19a0d82fe54dacda5a0b5dd1c8144cce0]

summary: Ensure that the WriteConcernError "errInfo" object is propagated
Support speculative authentication attempts in isMaster
description:
Clients will be able to specify a new argument to {{isMaster}} which speculatively includes the first {{saslStart}} command of an authentication handshake. This command may be provided to the server in parallel with any standard request for supported authentication mechanisms. This would permit clients to merge their {{saslStart}} message with their {{isMaster}} request, and receive the {{saslStart}} reply with the {{isMaster}} reply. Speculative authentication is ignored for server versions less than or equal to 4.2 and is supported for X.509, SCRAM-SHA-1 and SCRAM-SHA-256.

summary: Clarify how a driver must handle wrong set name in single topology
description:
The SDAM specification allows specifying replica set name in single topology (https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/server-discovery-and-monitoring/server-discovery-and-monitoring.rst#initial-setname). If this is done, the set name specified by the application must match the set name reported by the deployment. https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/server-discovery-and-monitoring/server-discovery-and-monitoring.rst#verifying-setname-with-topologytype-single says:

> A client MAY allow the user to supply a setName with an initial TopologyType of Single. In this case, if the ServerDescription's setName is null or wrong, the client MUST throw an error on every operation.

The Ruby driver satisfies this requirement by checking setName during server selection if the deployment is in single topology. However the prescribed server selection procedure in single topology (https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/server-selection/server-selection.rst#topology-type-single) does not mention such a check.

In order to improve consistency in how different drivers handle wrong setName when in single topology, either the SDAM or server selection specifications could either recommend or mandate a specific procedure that the drivers must follow when the set name of the deployment does not match the expected set name.

The specification changes for this are in this commit: https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/bc864e6f9ccbdd162658cfbc08bfb1cb713143c5

summary: Tests drivers raise error if hint specified for unacknowledged delete using OP_MSG or OP_DELETE

In addition, I would like to add tests for this functionality, as well as the related update functionality described in DRIVERS-790 (no tests were added for that change).

Maybe we can get around this by doing a deleteMany({}) instead of a drop?

CC [~kevin.albertson]

-------

Spec change: https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/0c6e56141c867907aacf386e0cbe56d6562a0614

SPEC-1669 updated the database from "admin" to "keyvault". Drivers will need to resync spec tests. Drivers testing client-side encryption on sharded clusters will need to resync to pass when running against 4.5.1+ servers due to SERVER-35804.

In addition to resyncing spec tests, drivers will also need to update prose tests to use a different key vault namespace.

CRUD tests in the original format (no {{object}} field or APM, and singular {{operation}} field) are now located in a "v1" sub-directory. Tests in the new format, which should be consistent with the Transactions spec (and others), are in a "v2" sub-directory.

summary: Rephrase unsupported/corrupt BSON messages
description:
Soon -- pretty very soon -- MongoDB will be introducing new BSON types.
Although old drivers may not be connecting to MongoDB versions using that BSON types, bson dump files and whatnot will eventually contain these types and these files shoved through old driver versions.

Currently most drivers fail with very confusing error messages, especially if you are actually connected to a MongoDB supporting these types.

This ticket is about unifying this error message to be more friendly and possibly alerting the user that newer drivers may support this type and the BSON may not actually be corrupt as currently reported.



{quote}
Detected unknown BSON type "\xTYPE" for fieldname "NAME". Are you using the latest driver version?
{quote}

Where
- {{TYPE}} is the current field type
- {{NAME}} is the current field name, if possible


The type name should be represented in hex confirming to our BSON Spec convention '\x00'.

The field name may not be possible to include in drivers, but drivers SHOULD make BEST EFFORT to include it. It helps our users a great deal as they could potentially project that field out as a poor mans workaround.
It is not required to provide the fieldname using dotnotation for nested fields. The inner most fieldname is sufficient.


Please ensure this is done for all currently actively - AND INACTIVELY - maintained drivers.

summary: Make allowDiskUse opt-out rather than opt-in in 6.0
description:
{panel:title=Original Downstream Change Summary|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
    In 6.0, the server will permit spilling to disk during query execution by default. If users want to explicitly prohibit disk use, they can pass {{allowDiskUse:false}} alongside {{find}} or {{aggregate}} commands.

This project also introduces a new {{setParameter}} called {{allowDiskUseByDefault}}. The default value of {{allowDiskUseByDefault}} is true. Operators can switch this parameter to false at startup or runtime on a per-node basis in order to restore the pre-6.0 behavior of prohibiting spilling to disk by default.
    h2. Description of Linked Ticket
    {panel:title=Epic Summary|borderStyle=dashed|borderColor=#cccccc|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
h4. Summary

Starting with 6.0, users queries which require greater than 100mb of memory for query working sets will automatically spill to disk. Users will be able to disable this behavior on a per-node basis.
h4. Motivation

Today, users must change their application code to enable a query to succeed when they reach an arbitrary working set limit for blocking sorts by permitting the server to spill to disk.  Starting in 6.0, we have added additional stages that may require spilling to disk, and educating our users about how to use them is unintuitive and lowers their value because users must explicitly opt in to the improved functionality.

Common stages that spill to disk are $group, $sort, $setWindowFields, bucket unpacking with sorting, and blocking sorts. In addition, $lookup will not consider a hash join without allowDiskUse enabled as it can spill to disk, an optimization that can provide orders of magnitude perf gains for non-indexed inner collections.
h4. Documentation

[Product Description|https://tinyurl.com/yb3wxpfw]
 [Scope Document|https://tinyurl.com/5n8vnn5m]
 [Technical Design Document|https://tinyurl.com/y87tuk9f]
{panel}

summary: Add `backgroundThreadIntervalMS` pool option to CMAP test specification and use it to avoid race conditions
description:
The specification changes: https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/ff282c752dc73ae49622df4ac87a68a9e9dbe95c.

h3. Original description

The test [{{pool-checkout-no-idle.yml}}|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/pool-checkout-no-idle.yml] has a race condition that can (and occasionally does) result in a [test failure|https://spruce.mongodb.com/task/mongo_java_driver_tests_jdk_secure__version~latest_os~linux_topology~standalone_auth~auth_ssl~ssl_jdk~jdk8_test_patch_ddefdb0cc29541f82d507318ef7f94ec03efcd39_608ededc3627e06f3974cb5a_21_05_02_17_18_20/tests?execution=0&sortBy=STATUS&sortDir=ASC] (a similar situations is with [{{pool-checkout-no-stale.yml}}|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/pool-checkout-no-stale.yml]). The test

# creates a pool with {{maxIdleTimeMS=10}}, populates the pool with a single connection (checks out and checks in);
# waits long enough (50 ms > 10 ms) for the connection to expire;
# checks out a connection and expects that the first one is closed as expired ({{ConnectionClosed}}) and a new one is created ({{ConnectionCheckedOut}}).

The problem is that this test does not take into account that the first connection may be discovered to be expired and closed by the pool's maintenance thread. For such executions, the event {{ConnectionClosed}} for the first connection and the event {{ConnectionCheckedOut}} for the second connection are concurrent.

While this particular test may be fixed by simply delaying the start of the maintenance thread task enough for the test to complete (can't do because the behavior is not standard; see this [Slack message|https://mongodb.slack.com/archives/G016S9LLKU2/p1620073778035400?thread_ts=1620071750.029400&cid=G016S9LLKU2] for the proposed solution), the problem with this test actually lays much deeper in the [CMAP|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/connection-monitoring-and-pooling.rst] and [CMAP test format and runner|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/README.rst] specifications. On one hand the CMAP specification does not require [CMAP events|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/connection-monitoring-and-pooling.rst#events] to be observable in the same order as the observed order of the pool operations{^}1^, on the other hand the test runner specification requires to [_"capture any Connection Monitoring events emitted in order"_|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/README.rst#unit-test-runner] (I struggle to understand what this requirement means given that the CMAP specification does not specify any partial order of events, not even mentioning that there is no total order) and to verify them in order _"for every idx/expectedEvent in events: Assert that actualEvents[idx] exists; Assert that actualEvents[idx] MATCHES expectedEvent"_.

----
{^}1^ As an example, let's consider a pool with [generation|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/connection-monitoring-and-pooling.rst#clearing-a-connection-pool] 0, and two [operations|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/connection-monitoring-and-pooling.rst#connection-pool] happening concurrently: {{clear()}} and {{checkOut()}}. Consider further an execution in which the checked out connection has its [generation|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/connection-monitoring-and-pooling.rst#connection-1] equal to 1. Based on this observation, the {{clear()}} operation appears to be ordered before {{checkOut()}}. But if we now look at the {{PoolClearedEvent}} and {{ConnectionCheckedOutEvent}}, then it is possible to observe them in a different order:
* threadA executing {{clear()}} increments pool's generation (the new value is 1);
* threadB executing {{checkOut()}} checks out a connection (possibly clearing all connections with generation 0) and sends {{ConnectionCheckedOutEvent}};
* threadA sends {{PoolClearedEvent}}.

Judging based on the observed events, the {{clear()}} operation appears to be ordered after {{checkOut()}}, which is the opposite of what we derived based on the observable generation of the checked out connection.

I think it's a good thing that not even partial order is defined for events because doing so has a potential to complicate the currently already quite complex pool's logic and implementation. For example, it would also with necessity require us to send some notifications while holding internal locks, which, given that users may specify their listeners, would result in us executing arbitrary code while holding an internal lock, which in turn, may result in deadlocking depending on the code in the user's event listener.

summary: Connection pools must be created and eventually marked ready for any server if a direct connection is used
Fix race condition in pool-clear-clears-waitqueue.yml
description:
The test [{{pool-clear-clears-waitqueue.yml}}|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/e6ae8bc82b23273a15232d2aa4727441482447a0/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/pool-clear-clears-waitqueue.yml] has a race condition that causes [occasional failures|https://evergreen.mongodb.com/test_log/6090a49bc9ec4455892514cd?text=true]:

# After [clearing|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/e6ae8bc82b23273a15232d2aa4727441482447a0/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/pool-clear-clears-waitqueue.yml#L31] the pool, the test [waits for three {{ConnectionCheckOutFailed}} events|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/e6ae8bc82b23273a15232d2aa4727441482447a0/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/pool-clear-clears-waitqueue.yml#L32].
# Then the test proceeds with checking the total order of all captured events, and expects that {{ConnectionPoolCleared}} is emitted before all three {{ConnectionCheckOutFailed}}.

The problem is that those 3 {{ConnectionCheckOutFailed}} events are emitted by threads {{thread1}}, {{thread2}}, {{thread3}} concurrently with the main test thread emitting {{ConnectionPoolCleared}}. This is because the specification states _"{{PoolClearedEvent}} MUST be emitted *after* incrementing the generation / marking the pool as "paused"._ As a result, the threads {{thread1}}, {{thread2}}, {{thread3}} learn that the pool is paused and emit {{ConnectionCheckOutFailed}} concurrently with the thread that cleared the pool emitting {{ConnectionPoolCleared}}.

The test can be fixed by waiting for {{ConnectionPoolCleared}} (does not matter whether we wait for three {{ConnectionCheckOutFailed}} events before or after doing this), and removing the {{ConnectionPoolCleared}} from [{{events}}|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/e6ae8bc82b23273a15232d2aa4727441482447a0/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/pool-clear-clears-waitqueue.yml#L36].

summary: [CMAP] A pool must emit PoolReadyEvent before resuming the background thread
description:
The test [{{pool-clear-min-size.yml}}|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/pool-clear-min-size.yml] has a race condition which causes a [test failure|https://spruce.mongodb.com/task/mongo_java_driver_tests_jdk8_unsecure__version~3.2_os~linux_topology~sharded_cluster_auth~noauth_ssl~nossl_jdk~jdk8_test_patch_ddefdb0cc29541f82d507318ef7f94ec03efcd39_608ededc3627e06f3974cb5a_21_05_02_17_18_20/tests?execution=0&sortBy=STATUS&sortDir=ASC] in the following execution:

# The test executor calls {{pool.ready()}}, which marks the pool as ready after clearing it (this is the [second {{ready}} operation in the test|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/tests/pool-clear-min-size.yml#L15]).
# The background thread is resumed as starts populating the pool to satisfy {{minPoolSize: 1}}. The background thread creates a new connection and emits {{ConnectionCreated}} event.
# The test executor continues executing {{pool.ready()}}, and sends {{ConnectionPoolReady}}.

Events {{ConnectionPoolReady}} and any of {{ConnectionCreated}}/{{ConnectionReady}} are concurrent, but the test expects them to always be emitted in the order {{ConnectionPoolReady}}, {{ConnectionCreated}}, {{ConnectionReady}}. In the execution specified above these events are emitted in a different order, which is almost explicitly allowed by the CMAP specification, which [states|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/70c26f323619573fba1e39a97cd5f2fa17dbaf3b/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/connection-monitoring-and-pooling.rst#marking-a-connection-pool-as-ready]:
{noformat}
mark pool as "ready"
resume background thread
emit PoolReadyEvent
{noformat}

A solution would be to explicitly wait for {{ConnectionPoolReady}}, {{ConnectionCreated}}, {{ConnectionReady}} in the test (the order is not important), and remove the second {{ConnectionPoolReady}} from the {{events}} section of the test (events {{ConnectionCreated}} and {{ConnectionReady}} are ordered for a connection). Another solution is discussed in this [Slack discussion|https://mongodb.slack.com/archives/G016S9LLKU2/p1620080361038900].

summary: Allow RewrapManyDataKeyResult.bulkWriteResult to be optional
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

Change the specification of {{RewrapManyDataKeyResult.bulkWriteResult}} from {{BulkWriteResult}} to {{{}Optional<BulkWriteResult>{}}}. Update corresponding unified test files to avoid unnecessarily matching {{bulkWriteResult}} against an empty document.
h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

Drivers implementing {{ClientEncryption.rewrapManyDataKey()}} and testing against the corresponding unified spec tests.
h4. How does this affect the end user?

The Client Side Encryption spec [currently defines|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/e3a59909621afa5d8b7795c19a0ba204f6b20b13/source/client-side-encryption/client-side-encryption.rst#rewrapmanydatakeyresult] the {{RewrapManyDataKeyResult.bulkWriteResult}} field as a {{{}BulkWriteResult{}}}. This is causing some confusion regarding what the value of {{bulkWriteResult}} should be provided {{ClientEncryption.rewrapManyDataKey()}} does not issue an unnecessary bulk write command due to [no matching key documents to rewrap|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/e3a59909621afa5d8b7795c19a0ba204f6b20b13/source/client-side-encryption/tests/unified/rewrapManyDataKey.yml#L92].
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

Users that have already implemented {{ClientEncryption.rewrapManyDataKey()}} and {{RewrapManyDataKeyResult}} may have to update their implementations to allow for the {{bulkWriteResult}} field to be optional.
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

If this change is not applied, Drivers may end up with discrepancies in interpretation and implementation of the behavior of {{rewrapManyDataKey()}} given the case where there are no matching key documents, thus no well-defined value for {{bulkWriteResult}} (an empty document may not be consistent or appropriate across all Drivers).
h4. Is this issue urgent?

No.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

No.
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

No. This may require changes to the implementation of {{RewrapManyDataKeyResult}} and corresponding operations that depend on it.
{panel}

summary: Ensure "does not fail" in CSE Prose Test 13 accounts for (no) matching documents
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

The current wording in [Client Side Encryption Prose Test 13|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/tree/dac4aebd2a5817d52ca286af4be1f12d54d35103/source/client-side-encryption/tests#unique-index-on-keyaltnames] requires certain {{addKeyAltName()}} operations "does not fail". However, this description does not take into account potential failure to find a matching document to add a {{keyAltName}} to, which also satisfies the "does not fail" condition (returning a null value instead of the previous state of the matched key document).

The prose test description should be improved to ensure the {{addKeyAltName()}} operations are succeeding because it successfully found and added a {{keyAltName}}  to a matching document, not because it did _not_ find a matching document.
h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

Drivers that have implemented, or will implement, CSE Prose Test 13.
h4. How does this affect the end user?

Drivers' implementation of CSE Prose Test 13 will need to assert the result of {{addKeyAltName()}} operations include the {{keyAltName}} added in a previous operation if they are not already doing so.
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

During implementation, Drivers might construct an incorrect filter argument to {{addKeyAltName()}} and fail to correctly test the success conditions intended by the prose test. This is more likely on Drivers that must convert to/from Native UUID Types.
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

Minor.
h4. Is this issue urgent?

No.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

No.
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

Yes.
{panel}

summary: Reduce expected removeKeyAltName operations to a single findOneAndUpdate
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

DRIVERS-2017 introduced unified spec tests for the Key Management API added to ClientEncryption in DRIVERS-1951. The unified spec tests for the {{removeKeyAltName()}} method, as currently written, expect up to two operations: a {{findOneAndUpdate()}} to remove a {{keyAltName}} from the {{keyAltNames}} field of a matching key document, and a conditional {{updateOne()}} to {{$unset}} the {{keyAltNames}} field if the array would otherwise be empty. This is to satisfy the conditions of the partial filter expression for the unique index on {{keyAltNames}} in the key vault collection.

The {{removeKeyAltName()}} function can be optimized to use only a single {{findOneAndUpdate()}} that still conditionally {{{}$unset{}}}s the {{keyAltNames}} field by using an aggregation pipeline.
h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

Any Drivers that have begun implementing, or have already implemented, the new  {{removeKeyAltName()}} function.
h4. How does this affect the end user?

Drivers will have to update the unified spec test file(s) and implementation of {{removeKeyAltName()}} to satisfy the new expectations.
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

N/A
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

N/A
h4. Is this issue urgent?

No. This is an optimization.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

No.
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

No. The updated unified spec test will also require updating existing implementations of {{{}removeKeyAltName(){}}}.
{panel}

summary: Investigate changes in SERVER-75121: Remove JWKS URI from server OIDC configuration
description:
{panel:title=Original Downstream Change Summary|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
    This change removes the JWKSUri from OIDC configuration and saslStart responses. The JWKSUri is discovered from a metadata endpoint which can be constructed from the issuer URI, which is already defined in the configuration.
    h2. Description of Linked Ticket
    SERVER-74735 must incidentally implement OAuth2 Authorization Server metadata discovery. We can re-use that mechanism to discover the JWKS endpoint, which we require in order to acquire the issuer's public token signing keys. Instead of requiring our administrator to populate the JWKS endpoint in our configuration, we should use metadata discovery to acquire the JWKS endpoint ourselves. Polling the endpoint will require network connectivity anyway, so this doesn't make us more brittle, and reduces configuration.

summary: Remove unnecessary on-demand credentials in CSE unified spec tests
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

On-demand credentials syntax was used in several CSE unified spec tests primarily out of convenience rather than necessity. This is causing unnecessary friction in Drivers that have yet to implement on-demand credentials. Unified spec tests that do not test on-demand credentials should be updated to avoid unnecessarily requiring it.
h3. *Motivation*

h4. How does this affect the end user?

h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

summary: Fix server version incompatibility for updateOne-let and updateMany-let server-side error tests
description:
[~bailey.pearson] observed that the [updateOne-let.yml|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/crud/tests/unified/updateOne-let.yml#L64] and [updateMany-let.yml|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/crud/tests/unified/updateMany-let.yml#L69] tests introduced in [mongodb/specifications@1519da9|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/1519da92c882fa902c365700bb2a1d21a6d66244] for DRIVERS-1788 use pipeline-style updates (4.2+) despite the tests specifying a minServerVersion of 3.6.0.

These tests should either have minServerVersion bumped to 4.2.0 or be changed to not use pipeline-style updates.

Per the docs examples in [Use Variables in let Option or c Field|https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/command/update/#use-variables-in-let-option-or-c-field], it looks like we can reference bound variables in the update's query by using [{{$expr}}|https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/operator/query/expr/] to access the aggregation context. This should be portable for pre-4.2 servers if we'd rather not bump the minServerVersion.

AFAIK, {{$expr}} cannot be used in a non-pipeline update, so we'd need to require 4.2+ if we still want to access the bond variables from the update itself (i.e. {{u}} parameter).

summary: Drivers should not create the ECC collection in v2 of queryable encryption
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

_Once the protocol changes in version 2 of queryable encryption have been made in the server, the ECC collection is no longer required when creating a new QE-encrypted collection. Thus, the drivers must update their QE helpers to no longer create the ECC state collection when creating an encrypted collection._
h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

_Users of the new QE v2 wire protocol._
h4. How does this affect the end user?

_Since the ECC collection is obsoleted in v2 of QE, this will reduce the number of state collections needed for QE to work._
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

_This occurs every time the user creates a QE-encrypted collection._
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

_If the server is using v2 of the QE protocol, and the driver is still creating ECC collections, then those ECC collections are never going to be used, and will only serve to clutter the database._
h4. Is this issue urgent?

_This should be implemented once PM-2972 is complete._
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

_n/a_
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

_no_
{panel}

summary: Add decimal128 clamped zeros tests with very large exponents
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

The Go driver recently fixed a bug that could cause an effectively infinite loop when parsing {{decimal128}} Extended JSON values that contain extremely large positive or negative integers (see GODRIVER-1519). We should add tests to the BSON corpus that check for similar bugs in other drivers.
h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

Customers who want to parse {{decimal128}} values from Extended JSON strings or other strings.
h4. How does this affect the end user?

The parser may hang indefinitely or behave unexpectedly when clamping certain values with very large positive or negative exponents.
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

The problem only occurs when parsing specific strings as {{{}decimal128{}}}. Examples include:
 * "0E999999999999"
 * "0E-999999999999"

An Extended JSON marshaler that passes the BSON test corpus should never generate the problematic strings, so the problem is only likely to happen if a customer uses the string-to-{{decimal128}} parser to parse user-provided input.

h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

The customer's application could hang. If the customer's application parses user-provided input, it could expose the customer to a denial-of-service attack.

h4. Is this issue urgent?

No.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

No.
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

Yes.
h3. *Acceptance Criteria*
 * Add decimal128 Extended JSON parse tests for clamped zeros with very large exponents.{panel}

summary: Add support for GCP attached service accounts when using GCP KMS
description:
We have a customer on GCP, who is trying to use GCP KMS for the CMK and we require a service account key, where an {{email}} and {{privateKey}} is provided for the service account, in order to use the GCP KMS API. They are using an [attached|https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/impersonating-service-accounts#binding-to-resources] service account according to [GCP best practices|https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/how-to-authenticate-service-accounts-to-help-keep-applications-secure] which says "*_Use attached service accounts when possible_*_. For applications deployed on Google Cloud that need to use a service account, attach the service account to the underlying compute resource. By attaching a service account, you enable the application to obtain tokens for the service account and to use these tokens to access Google Cloud APIs and resources_." and it also says "*_Use service account keys only if there is no viable alternative_*". Google even displays the warning "_Service account keys could pose a security risk if compromised_" when creating a key for a service account. 

The customer is requesting that we follow GCP best practices and when using an "attached" service account, that we skip/bypass the email and privateKey that is used for authentication and just access the GCP KMS API directly.

For reference https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices-for-using-and-managing-service-accounts#use-attached-service-accounts

The customer is running on CloudRun and sometimes Compute Engine on GCP so use attached service accounts. Their service accounts do not have user accessible keys on them.

The customer is using the MongoDB C#/.NET driver on Linux.

 During local development they use an environment variable GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS that if you point it to a local service account file (purely for development purposes) that means the Google libraries behave exactly the same as when deployed in a attached service account environment.

summary: Support the Azure VM-assigned Managed Identity for Automatic KMS Credentials
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

_At present, using Azure Key Vault for KMS requires a tenant ID, client ID, and client secret (password) in order to obtain an OAuth2 token to subsequently query the Azure Key Vault for key management._

_Azure VMs are automatically assigned a Managed Identity which allows the VM to obtain an OAuth2 token by querying a private metadata HTTP endpoint without any special credentials. The token obtained can then be used to request access to Azure resources, including the Key Vault, if applicable._

_We have added support for automatic KMS credentials for AWS (DRIVERS-2280) and GCP (DRIVERS-2377). The Azure VM Managed Identity can be used for the same purpose, removing the need for the client to provide credentials if they are running within an Azure VM that has the appropriate access to the Key Vault:_
 # _Allow the user to omit Azure credentials as part of {{{}kmsProviders{}}}, and request that credentials be obtained automatically from the environment._
 # _Attempt to load OAuth2 token from the Azure metadata endpoint. If we are running within an Azure VM, this token corresponds to the managed identity of that VM._
 # _Use the token from (2) as the KMS credential for client encryption._

h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

_All end users of client-side encryption that wish to use Azure Key Vault as their KMS provider, and are running their client within an Azure VM._
h4. How does this affect the end user?

_Supporting the VM's managed identity alleviates the need to manage a separate set of credentials for their client application._
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

_As Azure KMS users are likely running within an Azure VM, most Azure users will likely find benefit in delegating the credential management to the Azure platform._
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

_Managing and securing additional credentials creates an additional security concern and barrier to adoption for client-side encryption, whereas requesting the client driver to automatically work with the Azure host is simpler, more secure, and less error-prone._
h4. Is this issue urgent?

_The first mention of using Azure Managed Identities appears in December of 2020. The recent addition of automatic credentials for AWS and GCP makes this change prudent to match platform support._
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

_No_
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

_No_
{panel}

summary: Add test that reads are not retried in a transaction
description:

Add a missing test case to assert that a driver does not retry a read when in a transaction. This would have prevented JAVA-4684 regression.

The requirement is in the retryable reads specification in the [when not to allow retry|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/retryable-reads/retryable-reads.rst#a-when-not-to-allow-retry] section.

h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

Applications won't get the benefit of the convent transaction API's automatic retry mechanism if a driver has this bug
h4. Is this issue urgent?

Yes, since we want to fix the Java driver bug and need a test to assert correct behavior.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

No

It's a missing test of required behavior
{panel}

summary: Astrolabe - Clusters are failing to start
AWS Lambda metadata detection logic is too permissive
description:
DRIVERS-2209 outlined that if the AWS_EXECUTION_ENV env var is present, the app is running on AWS Lambda. This turns out to be too permissive because AWS_EXECUTION_ENV is used in other non-Lambda envs. For example the Windows evergreen machines have AWS_EXECUTION_ENV=EC2.

According to [Lambda env var docs|https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/configuration-envvars.html], I believe the driver needs to validate that the AWS_EXECUTION_ENV var begins with the  "AWS_Lambda_" prefix:
{quote}
- AWS_EXECUTION_ENV – The runtime identifier, prefixed by AWS_Lambda_ (for example, AWS_Lambda_java8). This environment variable is not defined for custom runtimes (for example, runtimes that use the provided or provided.al2 identifiers).
...
- AWS_LAMBDA_RUNTIME_API – (Custom runtime) The host and port of the runtime API.
{quote}

summary: Errata in pseudo code for retryable reads specification
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

_What is the problem or use case, what are we trying to achieve?_

It has been discovered that the [pseudo-code|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/d1157f7e135b5a98c50a1cc342597a0905a99a58/source/retryable-reads/retryable-reads.rst#pseudocode] provided in the documentation for the retryable reads is not aligned with the specified behavior of prose specification.

The specified prose specification behavior, in short, is as follows:
 # If the initial request fails, a retry attempt is initiated.
 # If this second attempt fails, we verify if CSOT is enabled, and subsequently perform another, second attempt. In case CSOT is not enabled, an error is expected to be thrown.

However, the [pseudo-code|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/d1157f7e135b5a98c50a1cc342597a0905a99a58/source/retryable-reads/retryable-reads.rst#pseudocode] within the specification suggests a different approach. It currently demonstrates only one *{{executeCommand}}* followed by a timeout check leading to indefinite number of attempts if CSOT is not enabled.

This situation appears to be similar to what was previously addressed in the retryable writes specifications DRIVERS-2695.
h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

_Who are the stakeholders?_
This affects the readers of the specification because the pseudo code disagrees with the prose spec.
h4. How does this affect the end user?

_Are they blocked? Are they annoyed? Are they confused?_
This is not a blocker – rather a point of confusion.
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

_Main path? Edge case?_

This problem will only occur for driver developers who are very closely inspecting the specification, perhaps during implementation or when debugging this path in a driver.
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

_Minor annoyance at a log message? Performance concern? Outage/unavailability? Failover can't complete?_

Worst case scenario is inconsistencies in how drivers handle retryable reads due to ambiguities in the pseudo code for the specification.
h4. Is this issue urgent?

_Does this ticket have a required timeline? What is it?_

Not urgent, no timeline.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

_Needed by e.g. Atlas, Shell, Compass?_

No
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

_Does this ticket have any functional impact, or is it just test improvements?_

No, just the specification pseudo code.
h3. *Acceptance Criteria*

_What specific requirements must be met to consider the design phase complete?_

The prose spec and the pseudo code spec need to logically agree with each other.
{panel}

summary: Remove slaveOK connection string option from URI parsing
description:
Many of our drivers still parse {{slaveOk=true}} and interpret it as {{readPreference=secondaryPreferred}}. As part of removing oppressive language, we need to remove or rename terms like {{slaveOk}}. Since there is already an equivalent connection string option, we are opting to remove connection string parsing of {{slaveOk}}.

Note that the {{slaveOk}} connection string option has been deprecated since before MongoDB 2.2. The last mentions of the {{slaveOk}} connection string option are in the [MongoDB 2.2 Connection String guide|https://docs.mongodb.com/v2.2/reference/connection-string/] and [MongoDB 2.4 Connection String guide|https://docs.mongodb.com/v2.4/reference/connection-string/] stating that {{readPreference}} would override {{slaveOk}} if both were present. Neither connection string guide documented {{slaveOk}} as a valid URI option.

Given that {{slaveOk}} has long been deprecated and alternatives provided since at least  MongoDB 2.2, we should remove this in the next non-patch release of our drivers.

summary: Support for $out to Time-series collections
description:
{panel:title=Original Downstream Change Summary|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
    These changes add a new option ("timeseries") for the $out aggregation stage. $out will now accept a "timeseries" option that takes in a time-series specification. $out will create a new time-series collection in the specified namespace if an existing collection does not exist in that namespace. If a collection exists in that namespace, $out will do the following: 

1. If the collection is not a time-series collection, $out will error.
2. If the collection is a time-series collection $out will error if the timeField and the metaField have been changed by the user. The time-series specification inputted in $out must match the existing time-series collection.
3. If the collection is a time-series collection and matches the specification passed in by the user $out will replace that existing time-series collection with the documents in the aggregation stage.

These changes are not supported for sharded collections. There is currently follow-up work tracked by [SERVER-74061|https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/SERVER-74061] currently in progress to add support for secondaries and sharded clusters.

For more information the syntax doc can be found at this link: https://docs.google.com/document/d/15lAB2yQ8e_fR4RnihjsJMotEaIiTe9q_qk-D509DWkM/edit?usp=sharing 
    h2. Description of Linked Ticket
    We should accept the time-series spec in the $out stage and use that to create a time-series collection as part of the {{DocumentSourceOut}} stage. This should remove any restriction prohibiting $out from targeting an unsharded time-series collection, and add tests that verify the correct behavior. We don't need to support sharded time-series collection as part of this ticket.

summary: Improving Time-Series Scalability
description:
{panel:title=Original Downstream Change Summary|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
    There will be some required changes to documentation on a few subjects, specifically collection creation options and indexing options for time-series collections. We are planning to sync up with the docs team when we complete the work for the new options and behaviors, since we're still ironing out the details during implementation.
    h2. Description of Linked Ticket
    {panel:title=Epic Summary|borderStyle=dashed|borderColor=#cccccc|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
h4. Summary

Add additional scalability features to better handle scalability challenges in Time Series collections and provide users with more flexibility options based on their use case such as:  

* Bucket re-opening to ensure better dynamic bucketing and support more use cases such as high cardinality or high cardinality with asymmetric ingestion rates  
* Fixed time-range bucketing to provide users with more storage modeling flexibility and better index usage for use cases that rely on fixed period roll-ups and do not need handling for overlapping buckets/out-of-order data to avoid overlapping buckets 
* A command to support rewrites for merge/split/consolidation of buckets 
* Unique indexing to support duplicate data protection at the database layer 

h4. Motivation

Time Series data by nature is demanding and challenging. While almost all users have time series use cases, creating a solution that generally supports these demands, challenges, and use cases with the scalability needed both dynamically and at the users control is something that will allow us to support many more customers requirements and move closer to a full-replacement TSDB

h4. Documentation

summary: Require hello command for connection handshake to use OP_MSG, disallowing OP_QUERY
description:
{panel:borderStyle=solid|borderColor=#21313c|titleBGColor=#21313c|titleColor=#fafbfc|bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

The [OP_MSG spec|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/b3b74c97c3b4258a74c77f27a6bffb520918b42a/source/message/OP_MSG.rst#usage] currently states the following:

{quote}
MongoDB drivers SHOULD perform the MongoDB handshake using OP_MSG if an API version was declared on the client, but MAY decide to use OP_QUERY.
{quote}

In addition, the [connection handshake spec|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/63b0451b4969f6bf7464e8d86d78b7552f65ba58/source/mongodb-handshake/handshake.rst#connection-handshake] states the following:

{quote}
If a server API version is requested, drivers MUST use the hello command for the initial handshake. If server API version is not requested, drivers MUST use legacy hello for the initial handshake and include helloOk: true in the handshake request.
{quote}

So, to reiterate:
* When an API version is _not_ requested, the driver is required to perform the connection handshake using an isMaster command (not hello) sent using the OP_QUERY RPC protocol (not OP_MSG).
* When an API version _is_ requested, the driver is required to perform the connection handshake using a hello command rather than isMaster. The spec advises that the hello command SHOULD be an OP_MSG, but does not require it.

The suggested change is specifically for the scenario when an API version _is_ requested. I propose that in this scenario drivers MUST use OP_MSG. Furthermore, we should specify that a driver MUST NOT send the hello command using the OP_QUERY RPC protocol, but rather MUST always send hello as OP_MSG.

h3. *Motivation*

This ticket is related to the work that the Query Execution team did recently to remove support for the legacy op codes from the server. In particular, the OP_QUERY RPC protocol is deprecated in version 5.0.0 and support for it will be removed completely in 5.1.0. However, there is still an [allowlist|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo/blob/7d222de143cd6ebb1d0613120d2219ba8e3ff41e/src/mongo/rpc/warn_deprecated_wire_ops.cpp#L115-L124] naming  commands that a 5.1 server will continue to accept as OP_QUERY commands. As of this writing, the allowlist consists only of:
* _isSelf
* buildinfo
* buildInfo
* hello
* isMaster
* ismaster
* saslContinue
* saslStart

These commands are the ones that either 1) drivers can send as OP_QUERY as part of the connection handshake, or 2) which internal client code in the server team's code base could send as OP_QUERY in version 5.0.

Over time we would like to narrow this allowlist. Once the allowlist is empty, the final traces of OP_QUERY can be removed from the code base.

h4. Who is the affected end user?

This change should be invisible to the end user of the driver. However, we should be careful about the error messages produced if the user attempts to set an API version, but the server predates API versioning. See related ticket DRIVERS-1619.

The goal of this ticket is to take steps towards the final eradication of the OP_QUERY RPC protocol. Doing so would permit additional simplification in the server code base, and eventually in the driver code bases as well.

h4. How does this affect the end user?

It doesn't. See above.

h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

This relates to the logic for implementing the connection handshake, so it affects the main path of all drivers.

h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

There are no negative consequences since the server will continue to support OP_QUERY hello commands for the foreseeable future.

h4. Is this issue urgent?

There is no particular urgency and no required timeline, but I believe it is valuable to keep paying down the remaining tech debt related to OP_QUERY.

h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

This contributes to our ability to get rid of OP_QUERY from the server team's code base once and for all.

This could also help to simplify the Atlas proxy, or any other proxy/server which drivers can connect to that is not maintained by the server team.

h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

This has a functional impact to how the connection handshake is implemented.

{panel}

summary: Use OP_MSG to authenticate if server supports OP_MSG
description:
*Background & Motivation*

Drivers must use OP_MSG to send commands for authentication during the MongoDB Handshake. SERVER-59085 will remove OP_QUERY support of the saslStart and saslContinue commands in MongoDB 6.0. saslStart and saslContinue commands are sent in drivers as part of the [authentication process|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/df6be82f865e9b72444488fd62ae1eb5fca18569/source/auth/auth.rst#sasl-mechanisms].

The process of [establishing a new connection|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/df6be82f865e9b72444488fd62ae1eb5fca18569/source/connection-monitoring-and-pooling/connection-monitoring-and-pooling.rst#establishing-a-connection-internal-implementation] involves:

1. Send an [initial handshake|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/df6be82f865e9b72444488fd62ae1eb5fca18569/source/mongodb-handshake/handshake.rst#connection-handshake].
2. Perform connection authentication if authentication is requested.

After (1), a driver knows if the server supports OP_MSG by inspecting the value of {{maxWireVersion}}.

Some drivers continue to use OP_QUERY for (2). That will be an error on 6.0 servers due to SERVER-59085.

This is a 6.0 requirement. SERVER-59085 plans to remove _isSelf/saslStart/saslContinue/buildinfo/buildInfo from the allowlist for OP_QUERY commands and is scheduled for 6.0.

*Scope*

Update the authentication specification to note that all authentication commands must use the wire protocol determined by the initial handshake. If the response to the initial handshake includes {{maxWireVersion}} greater than 6 (the [first version to support OP_MSG|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/df6be82f865e9b72444488fd62ae1eb5fca18569/source/wireversion-featurelist.rst]), all authentication commands must use OP_MSG.

Investigate whether it is practical to specify a test to validate this behavior. Failpoints cannot distinguish wire protocol versions. There may be no way to practically test this until SERVER-59085 is complete. After SERVER-59085 is complete, this will be tested in existing driver tests against the latest built servers (by virtue of authentication succeeding).

*Original Description*
{panel:title=Downstream Change Summary|titleBGColor=#239eb0|bgColor=#c2d2c2}
    The MongoDB shell cannot connect to 3.4 or older servers since we've changed DBClientConnection so that it sends "hello" or "isMaster" command as OP_MSG. DBClientConnection is also used by the repl code and the repl code bypasses wire protocol negotiation to send "_isSelf" command which is sent using the default wire protocol which is OP_MSG protocol now. Therefore, 5.1 mongod can't be interoperable with 3.4 or older mongod.

    {panel}

    h2. Description of Linked Ticket
    This is follow-on work to SERVER-58337. The OP_QUERY RPC protocol is deprecated in 5.0, so we can remove support for it in 5.1. As in the other related deprecation and removals (OP_INSERT, OP_KILL_CURSORS, OP_GET_MORE, etc.) we should continue to log a warning and bump the appropriate serverStatus counter prior to returning an error. Also, the error should be user-friendly, meaning that it should indicate that OP_QUERY commands, other than hello and isMaster, are no longer supported, as opposed to being some indecipherable error produced during validation of the incoming network message (e.g. "unknown op code 2004", or something similar).

Some additional info from DRIVERS-1877 (Remove _isSelf/saslStart/saslContinue/buildinfo/buildInfo from allowlist for OP_QUERY commands): When this is done, any driver that uses OP_QUERY for the entire handshake, instead of switching to OP_MSG after receiving a hello/ismaster reply indicating a 3.6+ wire version, will fail to connect.
Description from the Server linked to 1877: At the very late stage of PM-912 project, we realized that repl code is sending _isSelf/saslStart/saslContinue as OP_QUERY commands (see SERVER-58338) and some drivers are still using saslStart/saslContinue/buildinfo (or buildInfo) OP_QUERY commands as part of connection handshake (see SERVER-59069). Unfortunately, we could not change existing code and decided to allow those OP_QUERY commands.
We should remove _isSelf/saslStart/saslContinue/buildinfo/buildInfo OP_QUERY commands from allowlist until v6.0 release. To remove saslStart/saslContinue/buildinfo/buildInfo, we may need to coordinate the efforts with drivers team since some drivers are using them.

summary: Limit max simultaneous Astrolabe Atlas (cloud-qa) clusters
description:
{panel:borderStyle=solid|borderColor=#21313c|titleBGColor=#21313c|titleColor=#fafbfc|bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

Astrolabe creates Atlas clusters in the "cloud-qa" environment to test driver behavior during Atlas planned maintenance tasks. The Atlas "cloud-qa" environment limits the max number of simultaneous clusters we can create (max of 25; TODO: Figure out the real number?), and will either return an API error or take a very long time to create clusters (> 1 hour) if that number is exceeded. To prevent hitting that limit, we need to limit how many Astrolabe Evergreen tasks that create Atlas clusters can run in parallel.

Previously, we used the Evergreen distro "ubuntu1804-drivers-atlas-testing" that was specifically configured to only allow a max of 25 (TODO: Figure out the real number). However, there are two problems with that approach:
# That distro seems to have silently lost the important "max hosts" configuration at some point. That could happen again.
# We don't want to create special Evergreen distros every time we need to change to a different base distro.

Instead, we can use 
# Tag sets of tasks and make that set of tasks dependent on other setes of tasks. For example, label all of the Atlas tasks per driver language (e.g. "atlas-go", "atlas-java", "atlas-python", etc) and make each language group run after the previous language group (e.g. "atlas-go" depends on "atlas-java", "atlas-java" depends on "atlas-python", etc). That would effectively limit the parallelism to ~14 (which is how many Atlas tasks there are per driver).
See an example of how to create tag dependencies [here|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/blob/8eb74838db37d85b0051d9258af906b6e9ac44bf/.evergreen/config.yml#L2023].
# Put all Atlas tasks in an Evergreen [task_group|https://docs.devprod.prod.corp.mongodb.com/evergreen/Project-Configuration/Project-Configuration-Files/#task-groups]. Configure the {{task_group}} with {{max_hosts}} to limit parallelism (e.g. set it to 25). Note that a {{task_group}} changes the task run environment slightly, so could cause unexpected problems.

h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?
_Who are the stakeholders?_
h4. How does this affect the end user?
_Are they blocked? Are they annoyed? Are they confused?_
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?
_Main path? Edge case?_
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?
_Minor annoyance at a log message? Performance concern? Outage/unavailability? Failover can't complete?_
h4. Is this issue urgent?
_Does this ticket have a required timeline? What is it?_
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?
_Needed by e.g. Atlas, Shell, Compass?_
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?
_Does this ticket have any functional impact, or is it just test improvements?_
h3. *Acceptance Criteria*

* Astrolabe Evergreen builds limit the maximum simultaneous number of Atlas "cloud-qa" clusters to 25 or less.
* Astrolabe Evergreen builds still complete in a reasonable period of time (< 4 hours).
* Ideally, only "atlas-" tasks should be limited; "kind-" tasks should not be impacted.

{panel}

description:

Expectation for {{ "expectError": \{"isTimeoutError": true \} }} errors from {{withTransaction}} blocks is to propogate any errors.
h3. *Motivation*

The tests state:
{quote}# Because the second insert expects an error and gets an error, it technically succeeds, so withTransaction
# will try to run commitTransaction. This will fail client-side, though, because the timeout has already
# expired, so no command is sent.
{quote}
Errors from within {{withTransaction}} blocks should be propogated according to spec.

However, them "technically" succeeding really depends on the unified test runner implementations - so they may or may not issue an {{abortTransaction}} operation.

Yes

PR - review and acceptance by spec owner

summary: Disable causal consistency in implicit sessions
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Drivers Changes*
Drivers should sync the new unified sessions tests in [mongodb/specifications@5a15b65|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/5a15b65d6743a41ab2cb9270ba4c66dfda36bd7c]. Drivers that enable `causalConsistency` in *implicit* sessions by default will require code changes. Note that `causalConsistency` must continue to be enabled by default in *explicit* sessions.

h3. *Summary*

Read concerns "linearizable" and "available" cannot be used in causally consistent sessions (see documentation [here|https://www.mongodb.com/docs/v6.0/reference/read-concern-linearizable/#causally-consistent-sessions]; see server code [here|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo/blob/fdf1e87a30e8c879080839573700ceed42ab1db4/src/mongo/db/repl/read_concern_args.cpp#L234-L246]). However, the Causal Consistency spec [says|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/68d1095ef710ba294894474f7b8f3297c21b86f6/source/causal-consistency/causal-consistency.rst#causalconsistency] that causal consistency should be enabled in sessions by default unless {{{}snapshot=true{}}}. Enabling causal consistency in implicit sessions can cause server errors when a user sets read concern "linearizable" or "available".

A specific case where this causes a problem is with retryable reads because they reuse the same session and set an "operation time". As a result, a retried read will send a read concern document that includes {{{}afterClusterTime{}}}. If a user has also set the read concern to "linearizable" or "available", that retried read will fail with error {{InvalidOptions(72)}} with message:
{quote}afterClusterTime field can be set only if level is equal to majority, local, or snapshot
{quote}
Update the [Causal Consistency|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/68d1095ef710ba294894474f7b8f3297c21b86f6/source/causal-consistency/causal-consistency.rst] spec to prevent read operations that use implicit sessions from sending a read concern document with field {{afterClusterTime}} when field {{level}} is not "majority", "local", or "snapshot". We can accomplish that by either requiring that drivers set {{causalConsistency=false}} for all implicit sessions or for implicit sessions where the read concern is not "majority", "local", or "snapshot".

Open questions:
 # Does sending {{readConcern.afterClusterTime}} with a retried read make the read result more correct? If so, should we still require that drivers create implicit sessions with {{causalConsistency=true}} only if the read concern is "majority", "local", or "snapshot"?
 # Does using implicit sessions with {{causalConsistency=true}} for writes have any effect (positive or negative) on retryable writes?

h4. Example repro steps
 # Create a Client that uses read concern "linearizable".
 # Set a failpoint that returns error code {{ShutdownInProgress(91)}} for a "find" operation one time.
 # Run a "find" operation on a collection.

We expect that the "find" operation to be retried once and then successfully return results. What actually happens is the server responds the first time with the failpoint error {{{}ShutdownInProgress(91){}}}, then when the "find" is retried the server responds with error {{InvalidOptions(72)}} with message:
{quote}afterClusterTime field can be set only if level is equal to majority, local, or snapshot
{quote}
h4. Additional references to causal consistency that may require updating

The Retryable Reads specification mentions in question [Can drivers resend the same wire protocol message on retry attempts?|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/68d1095ef710ba294894474f7b8f3297c21b86f6/source/retryable-reads/retryable-reads.rst#can-drivers-resend-the-same-wire-protocol-message-on-retry-attempts]:
{quote}2. If the initial attempt failed with a server error, then the session's {{operationTime}} would be advanced and the next read would include a larger {{{}readConcern.afterClusterTime{}}}.
{quote}
If we chose to disable causal consistency in implicit sessions, retried reads will not send {{{}readConcern.afterClusterTime{}}}. We should update that answer section to describe the updated behavior.
h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

Users who set read concern "linearizable" or "available" and leave retryable reads enabled (enabled by default).
h4. How does this affect the end user?

Retried read operations will fail with server error {{InvalidOptions(72)}} with message:
{quote}afterClusterTime field can be set only if level is equal to majority, local, or snapshot
{quote}
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

If a user sets read concern "linearizable" or "available" and doesn't explicitly disable retryable reads, any retried read will return an error.
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

Retried read operations that may have succeeded will always return an error instead of a result. In affected drivers, retryable reads do not work with read concern "linearizable" or "available".
h4. Is this issue urgent?

This bug affects at least the Go driver and could affect other drivers as well. The Java driver sets {{causalConsistency=false}} for all implicit sessions and is not affected.

The bug in the Go driver affects the {{mongosync}} project and is moderately urgent. The team maintaining {{mongosync}} does have a workaround, which is to use explicit sessions for all operations that need to use read concern "linearizable" or "available" and set {{causalConsistency=false}} on the session.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

No.
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

No.
{panel}

description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}

Recent [discussion|https://mongodb.slack.com/archives/C05P3MKT23E/p1707947294657189] and observations have confirmed that only one listCollections operation is required in the context of the client-side encryption test scenario, contrary to the current test definition in [timeoutMS.yml|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/96c20fb5ea74b341b29bec6e3f859323b11580c4/source/client-side-encryption/tests/legacy/timeoutMS.yml#L48]. This adjustment is based on the understanding of libmongocrypt's caching behavior, which negates the need for a second listCollections operation. Additionally, the internal MongoClient is not configured with auto encryption, and therefore would not produce the listCollections event.

Engineers who implement and test client-side encryption functionality.
h4. How does this affect the end user?

Improper testing could result in unnoticed bugs.
h4. Is this issue urgent?

_No_
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

_Yes_
h3. *Acceptance Criteria*
 * *Update Test Definition:* Revise [timeoutMS.yml|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/96c20fb5ea74b341b29bec6e3f859323b11580c4/source/client-side-encryption/tests/legacy/timeoutMS.yml#L48] to specify that only one listCollections operation is expected during the test scenario, removing any requirement for a second operation.{panel}

summary: Stop requiring drivers to use Int64 for array indices
description:
{panel:borderStyle=solid|borderColor=#21313c|titleBGColor=#21313c|titleColor=#fafbfc|bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*
[Currently|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/0984b0942b9d8aaa11610184d0be16b27a263ec3/source/crud/bulk-write.md#result], the specification requires a driver to use `Int64` for indexes of models in the {{NamespaceWriteModelPair[]}} array:

* {{insertResults: Map<Int64, InsertOneResult>}}
* {{updateResults: Map<Int64, UpdateResult>}}
* {{deleteResults: Map<Int64, DeleteResult>}}
* {{writeErrors: Map<Int64, WriteError>}}

In Java, indexes of arrays are {{int}}, which means that {{Map<Integer, ...>}} makes sense in Java. In Rust, indexes of arrays are {{usize}}, which means, that {{HashMap<usize, ...>}} probably makes sense in Rust.

Let's explicitly allow a driver to choose the type that makes best sense for the driver.

h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?
The people who implement and maintain drivers, as well as end-users who may have to deal with a sub-optimal API.
h4. How does this affect the end user?
They can be annoyed by the sub-optimal API.
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?
Not applicable.
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?
It affects usability and may make implementing the API more difficult.
h4. Is this issue urgent?
It better be addressed before more drivers implement the new bulk write API.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?
No.
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?
No.
h3. *Acceptance Criteria*
The specification does not infringe on the driver devs ability to pick the optimal type for array indexes.
{panel}

summary: Suggest ways to model summary vs verbose bulk write results in the API, instead of requiring to choose from the predefined approaches
description:
{panel:borderStyle=solid|borderColor=#21313c|titleBGColor=#21313c|titleColor=#fafbfc|bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*
Currently, the specification requires a driver to choose from one of the proposed ways to model summary vs verbose bulk write results in the API: [_"Drivers MUST implement this in one of the following ways"_|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/0984b0942b9d8aaa11610184d0be16b27a263ec3/source/crud/bulk-write.md#summary-vs-verbose-results]. Let's use "SHOULD" instead of "MUST".

Let's improve the second proposed approach: _"Implement the insertResults, updateResults, and deleteResults fields as optional types and document that they will be unset when verboseResults is false."_ Making all these fields optional independently does not seem to make sense, because they are guaranteed to either all be present, or all be absent. Instead, the specification may propose to pack them in a single value, which always have these fields, while itself being optonal.

h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?
The people who implement and maintain drivers, as well as end-users who may have to deal with a sub-optimal API.
h4. How does this affect the end user?
They can be annoyed by the sub-optimal API.
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?
Not applicable.
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?
It affects usability.
h4. Is this issue urgent?
It better be addressed before more drivers implement the new bulk write API.
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?
No.
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?
No.
h3. *Acceptance Criteria*
The specification does not infringe on the driver devs ability to model API in a way that makes best sense for the driver.
{panel}

summary: Specs use insufficient markdown indentation for mkdocs
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*

The specifications use a flavor of Markdown which is compatible with GitHub-flavor but not with strict interpretations of the original spec as used by mkdocs.

In the specifications repository, nested lists use indentation levels of 2 or 3 spaces. The mkdocs interpretation requires 4 spaces for the nesting to be detected.

As a result, nested lists are collapsed leading to confusion about exactly where the nested items should be placed. In some cases, it isn't clear that the items should be nested at all.

Examples:

This is actually a set of four nested lists identifying separate test cases; mkdocs merges them into one flat list: [https://specifications.readthedocs.io/en/latest/bson-binary-uuid/uuid/#explicit-decoding]

The list of entities on the unified-test-format list is mostly flattened, meaning there's no way to immediately see which entity some fields are part of: [https://specifications.readthedocs.io/en/latest/unified-test-format/unified-test-format/#entity]  See the "database", "collection", and "session" entities. The last field in the previous entity and the field containing the next entity are only separated by a small amount of extra whitespace.

For more, try a regex search for /^ \{1,3}[-*0-9]/ in all markdown files.
h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?

Developers who use our public spec documentation, and view it via a local mkdocs installation or via readthedocs.
h4. How does this affect the end user?

Developer confusion in some cases.
h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?

Developers interested in low-level details will hit this quickly, but others may never refer to the specs.
h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?

The problem will be surprising/confusing at first, but once it's been identified there are easy workarounds: viewing the markdown source directly, or using a different processor like GitHub.
h4. Is this issue urgent?

No
h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?

No
h4. Is this ticket only for tests?

Documentation impact only.
h3. *Acceptance Criteria*

Nested text in existing specs should render as nested in the provided HTML version.

This could be accomplished by modifying the documents or switching HTML generators.

A naive replacement of all indentation leaves some lingering formatting problems, so it's possible a new HTML generator would be a more complete solution. Alternatively, there may be automatic formatting tools we could adopt.
{panel}

summary: Disable TLS renegotiation when possible
description:
TLS renegotiation is complicated, has been removed from TLS 1.3, and is not supported on the OS X and Windows native cryptography implementations. For consistency going forward, we should disable it on OpenSSL, if we are able to.

Some versions of OpenSSL define SSL_OP_NO_RENEGOTIATION, which disables renegotiation on TLS 1.2 and before. Drivers using OpenSSL should set the SSL_OP_NO_RENEGOTIATION flag on the SSL Context when defined.

summary: List all root-level document contexts and clarify when an expected array is an array of root-level documents
description:
{panel:borderStyle=solid|borderColor=#21313c|titleBGColor=#21313c|titleColor=#fafbfc|bgColor=#fafbfc}

h3. *Summary*
["Allowing Extra Fields in Root-level Documents"|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/unified-test-format/unified-test-format.md#allowing-extra-fields-in-root-level-documents] does not list all situations in which an expected document is to be treated as a root-level document that allows extra fields. Sometimes (maybe always) this is documented in places where pieces of test format are specified. For example, [{{expectedLogMessage}}|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/unified-test-format/unified-test-format.md#expectedlogmessage] specifies that test runners _"MUST treat the log message {{data}} as a root-level document"_.

Also, ["Allowing Extra Fields in Root-level Documents"|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/unified-test-format/unified-test-format.md#allowing-extra-fields-in-root-level-documents] mentions that an array of values may contain either ordinary documents or root-level documents. But it does not clearly state when it is one or the other.

h3. *Motivation*

h4. Who is the affected end user?
Driver maintainers.

h4. How does this affect the end user?
Driver maintainers are confused, and test runner implementations do not behave the same way:
* The Java driver does not implement matching as expected by the specification: JAVA-5629.
* The C driver also does not implement matching as expected by the specification. More specifically, it incorrectly treats {{expectResult}} in [{{expectedError}}|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/unified-test-format/unified-test-format.md#expectederror] as a root-level document that allows extra fields: https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/pull/1665#discussion_r1773998633.

h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?
Some drivers have already failed to implement the "rool-level document" logic correctly. Whether or not it is caused by the specification not being explicit/clear enough is unclear.

h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?
A test that should have failed may pass.

h4. Is this issue urgent?
No.

h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?
No.

h4. Is this ticket only for tests?
Yes.

h3. *Acceptance Criteria*
One can find an explicit criteria / list of situations when extra fields are allowed, including extra fields in documents in arrays.

{panel}

summary: Drivers: Exact Vector Search
description:
{panel:bgColor=#fafbfc}

Before State: Customers would benchmark using third party libraries, use HNSW even for small use cases, and have to use workaround strategies for multi-tenancy.

Developers looking to build retrieval augmented generation, semantic search, or recommendation systems with vector search who want to benchmark their systems, are working with a small scale of vectors, or have multi-tenant use cases.

summary: ConnectionId returned in heartbeats may be int64
description:
{panel:borderStyle=solid|borderColor=#21313c|titleBGColor=#21313c|titleColor=#fafbfc|bgColor=#fafbfc}
h3. *Summary*
In the {{hello}} response, the server will return {{connectionId}} as an {{int32}}, {{int64}}, or even {{double}}. Many drivers (and our specs) assume that it is an {{int32}}. This can result in connection failures in some drivers (e.g. .NET/C#) or truncation of the {{connectionId}} (Java).

h3. *Motivation*
h4. Who is the affected end user?
End users with long-running clusters and high connection churn.

h4. How does this affect the end user?
Depends on how the driver handles the overflow. Some will throw an exception. Others will truncate the {{connectionId}}. Worst case scenario, the user will be unable to connect to the MongoDB cluster.

Note that some drivers like Node.js and Python aren't affected by this bug because they use arbitrary-width numeric types.

h4. How likely is it that this problem or use case will occur?
This issue is relatively infrequent. To overflow an {{int32}} {{connectionId}}, the server would have to churn 100 connections per second for 8 months at a sustained rate. If the churn rate was higher, then the time to overflow would be proportionately shorter. This is mitigated by the fact that the server's {{connectionId}} counter is reset with every server restart.

h4. If the problem does occur, what are the consequences and how severe are they?
Some drivers (like .NET/C#) won't be able to connect until the affected server is restarted. Others like Java will simply truncate the {{connectionId}} making it difficult/impossible to correlate client and server logs. Others like Python and Node.js are unaffected by this bug.

h4. Is this issue urgent?
Given that restarting the affected server resolves the issue for multiple months even at high connection churn rates, this issue does not appear to be urgent at this time.

h4. Is this ticket required by a downstream team?
No.

h4. Is this ticket only for tests?
No.
_Does this ticket have any functional impact, or is it just test improvements?_

{panel}
